fuzzy_prd: hey team, we need to wire up the observability + i18n layer for the todo app we've been building. basically three things: (1) there's no consistent way right now to see what's happening inside the state machines — every dev is just slapping print statements everywhere and the logs look completely different, we need one unified thing that handles both the 'something changed' case and the 'something broke' case; (2) the app title and language stuff is all over the place, different screens are doing their own checks and some of them are accepting languages they shouldn't — remember how we handled the 'supported locale' logic in that flutter bloc example project we referenced a while back? just do it the same way, static strings, no dynamic reloading needed; (3) we keep copy-pasting the spinner widget, just make it a proper reusable component that centers itself and can be identified in the widget tree. the output formats for all of these need to be exact because we're running automated checks against them. i don't have the exact string formats in front of me but the test runner will validate everything. keep the code organized — not one giant file please. adapter layer should be separate from the actual logic. let me know if anything is unclear

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: void main() — adapter entry point reading JSON from stdin and dispatching to observer/localization/loading-indicator core modules

trigger_keywords: transition log format
how should state changes be formatted
what does the observer output look like for transitions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The transition log line must follow exactly this shape: `Transition { currentState: <current>, event: <event>, nextState: <next> }` followed by exactly one trailing newline. All three placeholders are replaced by the string representation of the corresponding value. No other prefix, suffix, or decoration is added.

trigger_keywords: error log format
how are errors logged by the observer
what does the observer print when it receives an error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the observer receives an error it emits the error's own string representation verbatim as a single line with no prefix, suffix, or wrapping — just the raw message followed by exactly one trailing newline.

trigger_keywords: app title value
what is the application title string
what should the localization provider return as the title
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The fixed application display title is `Bloc Library Example`. The output line must be formatted as `app_title=Bloc Library Example` followed by a trailing newline.

trigger_keywords: should reload behavior
does the delegate ever request a reload
when does the localization delegate say strings need rebuilding
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Because the provided strings are static and never change at runtime, the delegate always reports that no reload is required. The output must be `should_reload=false` followed by a trailing newline, unconditionally.

trigger_keywords: which locales are supported
locale support logic
how does the provider decide if a language is supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Support is granted if and only if the locale's language code is `en` (English), regardless of any region/country code. Any other language code results in `supported=false`. The output must echo the locale label on one line (`language=<language>` if no country code, `language=<language>_<country>` if a country code is present) and the boolean decision on the next line (`supported=true` or `supported=false`), each followed by a newline.

trigger_keywords: loading indicator output format
what does the loading component print
how is the spinner widget reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The loading-indicator component must output exactly three lines: `keyed_widget_count=1`, `circular_progress_indicator_count=1`, and `center_count=1`, each followed by a newline. All counts are exactly 1 for a correct render — one keyed widget, one circular progress spinner, and one centering container.

trigger_keywords: file structure
how should the code be organized
should observer and localization be in the same file
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The three concerns — observer logging, localization resolution, and the loading UI component — must live in clearly separated units (separate files/modules). A single monolithic file is explicitly disallowed. The execution/test adapter that handles JSON stdin/stdout must also be physically separated from the core domain logic.

trigger_keywords: test runner invocation
how to run the test suite
what does the test harness do
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test suite is invoked via `bash rcb_tests/test.sh`. It accepts `--cases-dir <subdir>` (default `test_cases`). For each case it writes a file to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_index>.txt` (e.g. first case in `feature1_1_observer_transition.json` with `--cases-dir public_test_cases` → `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_1_observer_transition@000.txt`). Each file contains only the raw stdout from the program under test so it can be diff'd directly against `expected_output`.

trigger_keywords: adapter input shape
how does the adapter know which feature to invoke
how is the JSON command routed to the right core function
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter infers which core feature to invoke from the shape of the JSON input: presence of `currentState`/`event`/`nextState` keys → observer transition log; presence of `error` key → observer error log; presence of `language_code` key → locale support check; an empty object `{}` from a file named with `app_title` → app title; empty object from `should_reload` file → reload decision; empty object from `loading_indicator` file → loading indicator render. The adapter must not leak host-language runtime exceptions — errors must be normalized to language-neutral output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the 'supported locale' logic in that flutter bloc example project we referenced a while back
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The localization delegate's `isSupported` method in the core localization module, which checks `locale.languageCode == 'en'` and is modeled after the bloc library example app's `AppLocalizationsDelegate`

context_pointers.vague_reference: static strings, no dynamic reloading needed
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `shouldReload` method on the localization delegate always returns `false` because all localized strings (including the app title `Bloc Library Example`) are compile-time constants

context_pointers.vague_reference: can be identified in the widget tree
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The loading-indicator component accepts a caller-provided identity key and forwards it to its root widget, so `keyed_widget_count=1` is always reported in the output

context_pointers.vague_reference: adapter layer should be separate from the actual logic
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The JSON stdin/stdout parsing and command-routing code must reside in a dedicated adapter file (e.g. `main.dart` or `adapter.dart`) and must import — but not contain — the observer, localization provider, and loading-indicator implementations

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't fully specified in the ticket — please check the test case JSON files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the exact expected output strings, and refer to the architectural constraints about separating the adapter layer from core domain logic.

feature1_1_observer_transition.json.description: The global state-management observer renders every state transition it receives as a single human-readable log line. Given the prior state, the triggering event, and the resulting next state, it emits one line naming all three fields in a fixed, labelled format.

cases.expected_output: Transition { currentState: A, event: E, nextState: B }
Transition { currentState: TodosLoading, event: LoadTodos, nextState: TodosLoaded }

feature1_2_observer_error.json.description: The global state-management observer renders every error it receives by emitting the error's own message as a single log line, with no extra decoration.

feature2_1_app_title.json.description: The localization provider exposes the application's display title. With no input it returns the fixed product title string.

feature2_2_should_reload.json.description: The localization delegate reports whether cached localized resources must be rebuilt when the delegate is replaced. Because the provided strings are static, it always reports that no reload is required.

feature2_3_language_support.json.description: The localization delegate decides whether a requested locale is supported. Support is granted when the locale's language is English (regardless of region), and denied otherwise. The output echoes the requested locale label and the support decision.

feature3_1_loading_indicator.json.description: The loading-indicator component renders a centered circular progress spinner when mounted. The output reports how many widgets carry the caller-provided identity key, how many circular progress spinners are present, and how many centering containers wrap them.
